natural_occurrence.description: Aluminium sulfate does not occur naturally in pure form, though related alum minerals exist. All commercial production is synthetic.

manufacturing.description: Produced by reacting aluminum hydroxide or bauxite ore with sulfuric acid. The solution is then crystallized to obtain various hydrated forms of aluminum sulfate.

other_uses.cosmetics: Astringent in some antiperspirants and styptic pencils (though potassium alum is more common).
other_uses.industrial: Water treatment (flocculation), paper sizing, dyeing mordant, fireproofing textiles.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Vaccine adjuvants (though usually as aluminum hydroxide or phosphate).
other_uses.household: Pickling cucumbers (in some traditional recipes), water purification.

eu.max_level_mg_kg: varies by category (typically 30-200 mg/kg as Al)
eu.conditions: Permitted in limited food categories with maximum residue limits. Primarily candied and crystallized fruits.

usa.notes: Limited to specific uses in food processing, particularly pickling.

japan.notes: Restricted use with maximum residue levels.

australia_nz.notes: Limited applications with maximum permitted levels.

canada.notes: Restricted use in specific food categories.

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: not specified (limited use in specific applications)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: no ADI established (limited use with maximum levels)

regulatory_history.event: EFSA re-evaluated aluminium-containing food additives, expressing concern about dietary aluminium exposure, particularly for children. Established maximum residue limits for specific applications.

commonly_found_in: Water treatment (not in the water itself, but used to purify it)
fun_facts: More commonly used to purify drinking water than as a food ingredient - clumps impurities together for removal
In old-fashioned pickle recipes, called 'alum' and used to keep pickles crisp and crunchy
Related to the 'alum' used in styptic pencils to stop bleeding from shaving cuts
Use in food has declined due to concerns about aluminum exposure, though science on health effects remains debated
The same chemical that makes paper smooth and resistant to ink bleeding through (paper sizing)
Historically used to make maraschino cherries firm and translucent before modern formulations
